Title: An NPF Transporter Exports a Central Monoterpene Indole Alkaloid Intermediate from the Vacuole

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Strictosidine is the central intermediate of monoterpene indole alkaloid biosynthesis leading to pharmaceutically important alkaloids including vincristine-related end products; excessive strictosidine accumulation is cytotoxic to plant tissue
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CrNPF2.9 exports strictosidine from the vacuole; silencing causes strictosidine accumulation and decreases downstream alkaloids vindoline and catharanthine, with associated leaf cell death
Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: NPF transporter family; nitrate/peptide transporter family member; contains N-terminal (D/E)X3-5L(L/I) tonoplast-targeting motif; lacks conserved EXXE(R/K) proton-coupling motif
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Virus-induced gene silencing in leaves; Xenopus oocyte uptake assay at pH 5; leaf epidermis-enriched transcript analysis
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq transcriptome co-expression analysis using a publicly available C. roseus dataset at medicinalplantgenomics.msu.edu
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Self-organizing map co-expression analysis; VIGS loss-of-function metabolite profiling by LC-MS; subcellular localization with YFP/CFP markers; epidermis-enriched qPCR; heterologous transport assay in Xenopus laevis oocytes
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CrNPF2.9 was identified by co-expression with known monoterpene indole alkaloid biosynthetic genes. 2. Silencing CrNPF2.9 in Catharanthus roseus caused a dramatic increase in strictosidine and a decrease in vindoline and catharanthine. 3. CrNPF2.9 localizes to the tonoplast and is enriched in leaf epidermis, matching the cellular context of strictosidine metabolism. 4. Heterologous assays showed CrNPF2.9 transports strictosidine with high affinity and specificity, consistent with vacuolar export.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Can be used to control intracellular flux and detoxification in monoterpene indole alkaloid engineering; transporter co-expression may improve production of strictosidine-derived alkaloids in heterologous systems by facilitating sequestration/export of toxic intermediates
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function causes extensive tissue necrosis/cell death due to strictosidine accumulation; transporter manipulation may alter downstream alkaloid balance
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper distinguishes CrNPF2.9 as the core experimentally validated transporter, while CrMATE1 and CrMATE2 were only tested as alternative candidates and not supported as causal core genes. CrNPF2.9 transports strictosidine along, not against, its concentration gradient.
